title: Price elasticity of supply
paperName: Cambridge International AS Level Economics (P1 & P2)
summary: 9708 AS PES — formula, determinants, and applications including tax incidence.
learningObjectives: Interpret elastic and inelastic supply diagrams.
Apply PES to tax incidence analysis.

simpleExplanation.title: How quantity supplied responds
simpleExplanation.summary: PES measures responsiveness of quantity supplied to a price change. Time and spare capacity are key determinants.

flashcards.back: PES = (% ΔQs) ÷ (% ΔP). Usually positive — higher price leads to higher quantity supplied.
PES > 1 — quantity supplied responds more than proportionately to a price change.
PES < 1 — quantity supplied responds less than proportionately; supply curve is steeper.

flashcards.front: Why is supply more elastic in the long run?
flashcards.back: Firms can adjust all factors — build capacity, enter/exit industry, retrain workers.

flashcards.front: How does PES affect tax incidence?
flashcards.back: The more inelastic side of the market bears a larger share of an indirect tax.
Fixed supply in the short run — e.g. seats in a stadium, perishable goods that must sell today.

sections.content: **PES** (2.3) pairs with **2.2 PED**. Together they determine **burden of indirect taxes**.
PES = (ΔQ/Q) ÷ (ΔP/P) | PES > 1 elastic | PES < 1 inelastic | Tax incidence: inelastic side pays more

sections.question:
When the price of wheat rises from $200 to $240 per tonne, quantity supplied increases from 500 to 550 thousand tonnes.

(a) Calculate PES.
(b) If an indirect tax raises the price consumers pay by $20, and supply is inelastic while demand is elastic, who bears most of the tax?
sections.solution:
(a) **Calculate PES**
ΔP = ($240 − $200)/$220 × 100 = **18.2%** (midpoint method)
ΔQ = (550 − 500)/525 × 100 = **9.5%**

PES = 9.5% ÷ 18.2% = **0.52** (inelastic — quantity responds less than price)

(b) **Tax incidence**
Supply is **inelastic** (PES = 0.52) and demand is **elastic** → **producers bear most of the tax**.

Reason: producers cannot easily reduce output, so they absorb much of the tax in lower post-tax revenue; consumers with elastic demand would buy much less if the full tax were passed on.

steps.caption: Inelastic: fixed capacity, perishable cannot store.
Tax incidence depends on PES vs PED.
